questions.passage:
The word representation carries a strain that its everyday use conceals. To represent is, in one sense, to make present again something that is absent, as a portrait represents a sitter or a map a territory. In another sense it is to act for others, to stand in their place and do on their behalf what they cannot do themselves. Political representation asks these two senses to coexist, and much of the confusion in democratic theory follows from the ways they pull apart.

Analysts have found it useful to separate several things the word can mean. A representative may be authorized, having been given by some procedure the right to act for others, quite apart from what he then does with that right. He may be accountable, obliged to answer afterward for his conduct, whether or not he was properly authorized at the start. He may be descriptive, resembling those he stands for in some salient respect, so that an assembly mirrors the population in its composition. Or he may be symbolic, standing for a people the way a flag stands for a nation, commanding a loyalty that has little to do with resemblance or procedure. These are distinct achievements, and a government may secure some while failing at others.

The deepest quarrel, however, concerns none of these directly. It is the old dispute over whether a representative should act as a delegate or as a trustee. The delegate view holds that he is a conduit for the wishes of those who sent him, bound to transmit their instructions faithfully and to substitute his own judgment for theirs as little as possible. The trustee view holds the opposite, that he owes his constituents not obedience but his best independent judgment, and that to poll them on every matter would be to abdicate the very office they elected him to fill. Each view can claim a democratic pedigree, and each, pressed to its limit, produces something most people would hesitate to call representation at all.

Push the delegate model far enough and the representative disappears, reduced to a messenger with no function his constituents could not perform by direct vote. Push the trustee model far enough and the constituents disappear, their wishes irrelevant between elections, replaced by a guardian who knows their interest better than they do. The one dissolves representation into mere transmission; the other inflates it into tutelage. That both extremes are unacceptable suggests that representation lives in the tension between them, not at either pole.

This is not merely a scholar's puzzle. The tension surfaces whenever a legislator votes against the expressed preference of her district on a matter she believes it has misjudged, and must decide whether she has betrayed her constituents or served them. It surfaces again in disputes over whether an assembly ought to look like the society it governs, a demand that treats resemblance itself as a form of representation, independent of what the resembling members actually do. A body composed to mirror a population may still act against its interests; a body that acts faithfully for a population may look nothing like it. Descriptive and substantive representation can each be present without the other.

No single formula resolves these strains, and the theorists who have thought longest about the concept tend to conclude that none should be expected to. Representation is not one relation but a family of them, held together by a common name and a shared problem, the problem of making a large and scattered people somehow present in the decisions that bind them. To ask which of its meanings is the true one is to misunderstand the concept. Its various senses are demands we place on the same institution, and the practical work of politics is the never-finished business of balancing them against one another.
questions.stem: The passage is primarily concerned with

choices.A: proving that the delegate model is the only defensible account of representation
choices.B: showing that political representation is a family of distinct and sometimes competing demands
choices.C: arguing that descriptive representation should replace all other forms
choices.D: recommending direct popular voting as a substitute for representatives

explanations.A: The author shows the delegate model, pushed far, dissolves the representative into a mere messenger, and treats it as one pole of a tension, not the sole defensible account.
explanations.B: Correct. The passage distinguishes authorization, accountability, descriptive, and symbolic senses, sets out the delegate-trustee dispute, and concludes that representation is not one relation but a family of them, held together by a shared problem. Mapping those competing demands is the central task.
explanations.C: Descriptive representation is presented as one sense among several that can be present without substantive representation, not as the form that should replace the others.
explanations.D: Direct voting is raised only to show what the delegate model collapses into; the author does not recommend it as a substitute for representation.

questions.stem: According to the passage, the delegate and trustee views differ chiefly over

choices.A: whether an assembly should mirror the population in its composition
choices.B: whether representatives should be elected or appointed to office
choices.C: whether a representative should transmit constituents' instructions or exercise independent judgment
choices.D: whether genuine representation requires prior authorization by procedure or only later accountability for conduct

explanations.A: Mirroring the population belongs to the descriptive sense discussed separately; it is not what divides delegate from trustee.
explanations.B: Election versus appointment is not the axis of the delegate-trustee dispute; both models assume an elected representative and differ over how he should then act.
explanations.C: Correct. The passage defines the delegate as a conduit bound to transmit constituents' instructions and the trustee as owing his best independent judgment rather than obedience. The disagreement is exactly this: transmit wishes or judge for oneself.
explanations.D: Authorization and accountability are two of the distinct senses catalogued earlier; the delegate-trustee quarrel concerns conduct in office, not the timing of authorization.

questions.stem: In stating that each model 'pressed to its limit' yields something few would call representation, the author aims to

choices.A: establish that the trustee model of independent judgment is clearly superior to the delegate model of instruction
choices.B: prove that neither model has any legitimate democratic pedigree
choices.C: recommend abandoning the concept of representation altogether
choices.D: show that representation lives in the tension between the two extremes rather than at either pole

explanations.A: Both extremes are judged unacceptable; the passage does not crown the trustee model superior but places representation between the two.
explanations.B: The passage grants that each view can claim a democratic pedigree; the point is that each fails only when pushed to its limit, not that both lack legitimacy.
explanations.C: The author retains the concept and calls balancing its senses the practical work of politics, so abandonment is not the aim.
explanations.D: Correct. The author says the delegate extreme dissolves representation into transmission and the trustee extreme inflates it into tutelage, concluding that both extremes being unacceptable suggests representation lives in the tension between them, not at either pole.

questions.stem: The example of a legislator who votes against her district's expressed preference is offered to illustrate

choices.A: the practical form the delegate-trustee tension takes in an actual decision
choices.B: the impossibility of ever knowing a district's true preferences
choices.C: the superiority of symbolic over substantive representation
choices.D: a case where authorization and accountability necessarily conflict

explanations.A: Correct. The author introduces the wayward vote to show the tension is not merely a scholar's puzzle, since the legislator must decide whether voting against expressed preference betrays or serves her constituents, exactly the delegate-trustee dilemma in practice.
explanations.B: The example assumes the district's preference is known and expressed; the difficulty is what to do about it, not whether it can be discerned.
explanations.C: The illustration concerns judgment versus instruction, not a ranking of symbolic against substantive representation, which the passage treats elsewhere.
explanations.D: Authorization and accountability are not the axis here; the example dramatizes the delegate-trustee conflict, a separate distinction.

questions.stem: The passage implies that a legislative assembly built to mirror its population

choices.A: will necessarily and in every instance act in that same population's genuine long-term best interest
choices.B: cannot be authorized by any democratic procedure
choices.C: may still act against the interests of the people it resembles
choices.D: is the only genuine form of representation the author endorses

explanations.A: The author denies this, warning that a body composed to mirror a population may still act against its interests; resemblance does not guarantee faithful action.
explanations.B: Nothing suggests a mirroring assembly cannot be authorized; the point is that resemblance and faithful action are independent, not that such a body lacks authorization.
explanations.C: Correct. The passage states plainly that a body composed to mirror a population may still act against its interests, and that descriptive and substantive representation can each be present without the other.
explanations.D: The author treats descriptive representation as one demand among several, not as the single genuine form endorsed above the rest.

questions.stem: The tone of the passage is best characterized as

choices.A: despairing, concluding that representation is an incoherent notion
choices.B: indignant, condemning existing representative institutions as fraudulent
choices.C: celebratory, praising representation as a solved achievement of modern democracy
choices.D: measured and analytical, mapping distinctions without championing one

explanations.A: The author explicitly holds the concept together as a family of relations sharing a common problem, rejecting the idea that it is incoherent.
explanations.B: There is no indignation or charge of fraud; institutions are analyzed, not condemned.
explanations.C: The passage calls the balancing of demands never-finished, hardly a celebration of representation as solved.
explanations.D: Correct. The author separates the senses of the word, lays out the delegate-trustee dispute even-handedly, and concludes that balancing the concept's demands is never-finished work, adopting the detached, distinction-drawing manner of analysis rather than advocacy.
